user_input.topic: How Singapore can stay competitive by embracing Artificial Intelligence
user_input.content:
Theme 1: We are entering a new era with AI
Points: None
Facts:
In 2020, AI struggled to solve basic math problems reliably, but by 2025 it could compete in the International Math Olympiad and achieve a gold medal-level performance.
AI is already widely used by Singaporeans, including for travel planning where it can recommend destinations and generate personalised itineraries.
In Singapore's civil service, AI is used in agency call centres to transcribe conversations in all four official languages and generate summaries so officers can focus on serving the public.

Theme 2: We need to harness the potential of AI to raise productivity and create new value through every part of the economy, keeping our nation competitive
Points:
Singapore's ability to combine technology and human expertise attracts high-quality investments and creates good jobs.
AI adoption is not limited to large companies, SMEs can also benefit.
Facts:
AI is being combined with robotics and autonomous machines at Tuas Port and studied for automation at Changi Airport.
GE Vernova's turbine repair facility in Singapore uses AI to detect anomalies in turbine components and alert human operators for precise diagnosis.
SMEs like Q&M Dental use AI to analyse dental X-rays to help detect fillings, impacted teeth, and tooth decay more accurately.

Theme 3: Many people are concerned about job displacement due to AI, but there are opportunities from technological change
Points:
Technological change in the past created disruption but also enabled workers to move into higher-skilled and better-paying jobs.
Facts:
In the 1980s, Singapore launched a nationwide computerisation drive that equipped students and workers with computer skills and transformed the economy. When the internet arrived in the 1990s, Singapore successfully adopted it and propelled the nation forward.
At PSA, crane operators were retrained to remotely supervise and manage multiple cranes and vehicles, boosting productivity and improving job quality.

Theme 4: Even as we embrace AI and technology, Singaporeans will always be our key priority
Points:
The government will work closely with NTUC and unions to redesign jobs and equip workers with new skills.
Efforts to help Singaporeans access jobs include expanded job matching services, more career fairs in tertiary institutions, and a new town-level job matching initiative led by CDCs.
The SkillsFuture Level-Up Programme will expand to include more part-time training allowance claims and more courses from industry leaders and private training providers.
Facts:
The SkillsFuture Level-Up Programme provides Singaporeans aged 40 and above with $4,000 in SkillsFuture credits and training allowances of up to $3,000 per month for up to 24 months for full-time study.
Vinoth Nanda Kumaran, aged 41 in 2025, transitioned from private hire driver to Business Intelligence Developer after using SkillsFuture credits to pursue a diploma in data engineering and continuing his studies in Business Analytics at SUSS.

Theme 5: Embracing AI is in line with Singapore's broader strategy of building a dynamic, innovative economy that creates good jobs
Points:
A dynamic economy is one that creates good jobs and better lives for all Singaporeans.
Embracing AI supports Singapore's goal of staying globally competitive.
Singapore aims to remain ahead through ideas, innovation, and unity.
Facts: None

stylistic_script:
Good evening, everyone.

Let’s start with an observation: AI used to struggle with the simplest tasks. Now? It’s everywhere in Singapore—changing how we travel, how government services work, and more. In 2025, AI models from Google and OpenAI scored gold medals at the International Mathematical Olympiad, a contest designed for expert human reasoning. That’s no small feat. Meanwhile, a study found that 55% of Singaporean travellers are already turning to AI to plan trips—getting personalised itineraries and activity suggestions tailored just for them. And in government call centres, GovTech’s ‘Transcribe’ platform automatically transcribes conversations in all four official languages plus Singlish. That frees up public officers to focus on what really matters: serving you better. From barely functional tools to powerful helpers integrated into daily life—that’s the AI journey here. This rapid evolution sets the stage for how AI is boosting productivity and creating new value across industries in Singapore.

Now, let’s get real about what this means for our economy and jobs. Take GE Vernova’s turbine repair facility here—they use AI to spot anomalies in turbine parts and alert human operators for precise checks. It’s not just the big players getting smarter; SMEs like Q&M Dental analyse dental X-rays with AI to detect fillings, impacted teeth, and decay more accurately than ever before. Then there’s Tuas Port—the world’s largest fully automated container-handling port—where AI and robotics push throughput and efficiency to new levels. Closer to home, Changi Airport is trialling AI-powered security systems that speed up checks by as much as 50%, plus deploying autonomous baggage tractors that boost tarmac productivity significantly. What does this tell us? Singapore’s unique mix of tech and human skill attracts quality investments—and good jobs—to our shores. And it’s not just giants benefiting; SMEs are catching on fast. But let’s not kid ourselves—AI also raises tough questions about job displacement. We have to face those head-on.

History shows us how technological change shakes things up but also opens doors. Back in the 1980s, Singapore launched a nationwide computerisation drive that equipped students and workers with computer skills—and transformed our economy. Today at PSA, crane operators aren’t just moving cranes—they’ve been retrained to remotely supervise multiple cranes and vehicles at once, boosting productivity while improving job quality. Meanwhile, the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) and the Institute of Banking and Finance (IBF) have teamed up with 11 leading financial institutions—including DBS, OCBC, and UOB—to pilot workforce transformation and upskilling initiatives preparing workers for an AI-enabled future. The message is clear: yes, disruption happens—but it also pushes workers into higher-skilled, better-paying roles. Building on this track record, Singapore is committed to supporting workers through this AI transition with targeted initiatives.

And we’re not just talking—we’re putting real support on the ground. The SkillsFuture Level-Up Programme gives Singaporeans aged 40 and above $4,000 in SkillsFuture credits plus training allowances of up to $3,000 per month for full-time study lasting up to 24 months. Take Vinoth Nanda Kumaran: at 41 in 2025, he switched from being a private hire driver to a Business Intelligence Developer after using SkillsFuture credits for a diploma in data engineering—and he didn’t stop there; he continued studying Business Analytics at SUSS. Beyond individuals like Vinoth, NTUC partners with companies through Company Training Committees (CTCs), providing grants covering up to 70% of costs related to AI adoption—including job redesign and skills training—with unions closely involved in these efforts. On top of training support, job matching services are expanding significantly—with Community Development Councils (CDCs) now leading town-level initiatives that connect job seekers directly with vacancies close to home—making it easier than ever to find work nearby. This tight collaboration between government agencies, unions, employers—and you—is how we make sure no one gets left behind as jobs evolve.

With these strong supports in place, Singapore is ready—not just willing—to embrace AI for a dynamic and competitive future.

Why does this matter? Because according to a 2025 Salesforce study, Singapore ranks second globally in AI readiness—excelling across regulatory readiness, actual AI adoption on the ground, and investment environments that attract serious players. Under our National AI Strategy 2.0, we’re investing over S$1 billion over five years to boost public AI research and development capabilities—strengthening our position as a global leader in this space. A key goal? To more than triple our AI practitioner workforce—to 15,000 strong—and establish Singapore as a global hub for creators shaping tomorrow’s technologies.

Bottom line: a dynamic economy powered by AI creates good jobs—and real opportunities—for Singaporeans who are ready to step up or adapt. We’re not chasing shiny trends blindly; we’re leading pragmatically through ideas, innovation—and yes—unity too.

Thank you.

judge_a_result.overall_summary: The speech is solidly on brief, fact-rich, and easy to follow, with a clear public-facing case for AI adoption in Singapore. Its main limitation is that it remains quite generic and presentation-like for a National Day Rally speech, with only moderate rhetorical lift and only partial resemblance to Lee Kuan Yew’s hard-edged, combative, pragmatic style.

task_audience_alignment.justification: It clearly addresses how Singapore can stay competitive through AI, speaks to Singaporeans in accessible terms, and covers reassurance, jobs, and national competitiveness appropriate for a National Day Rally. It fits the occasion reasonably well, though it lacks some of the political gravitas and emotional breadth expected for a high-stakes national address.

narrative_coherence.justification: The speech follows the intended arc well: rapid AI change, productivity gains, job concerns, worker support, and national vision. Transitions are explicit and logical. However, the structure is somewhat programmatic, with each section neatly signposted rather than building toward a more powerful cumulative argument.

content_quality_credibility.justification: It includes many specific, relevant examples and grounded facts from the blueprint, spanning public services, industry, retraining, and national strategy. This gives the speech credibility and breadth. Still, the treatment is more illustrative than deeply analytical, and some examples are presented in a list-like way rather than being more fully interpreted or synthesized.

clarity_fluency.justification: The speech is clear, readable, and generally concise, with strong sentence-level fluency and little ambiguity. The progression is easy to follow. Some phrasing feels slightly over-signposted or repetitive, and a few lines sound more like briefing prose than polished oratory.

engagement_ted_style.justification: The speech has a clean opening observation, some useful contrasts, and a few direct audience-facing lines. But it is only moderately memorable. Much of the speech reads like a competent policy explainer rather than a compelling, high-impact rally speech, with limited emotional resonance, rhythm, or standout phrasing.

voice_style_fidelity.justification: From the raw example, the speaker’s style is hard-headed, combative, pragmatic, plainspoken, and often adversarial, with forceful contrasts, concrete examples, blunt judgments, and a relentless problem-consequence-solution logic. The final speech captures some pragmatic and direct qualities, especially in lines like 'let’s get real' and 'let’s not kid ourselves,' but overall it is much smoother, more generalized, and more TED-like than the source style. It lacks the speaker’s distinctive toughness, argumentative sharpness, and muscular cadence.

strengths: Strong alignment with the requested topic, audience, and policy purpose
Logical structure with clear transitions from opportunity to concern to support to vision
Good use of specific Singapore-based examples across government, large firms, SMEs, and workforce policy
Clear and accessible language suitable for a general public audience
Credible emphasis on balancing competitiveness with worker support
weaknesses: Rhetorically flatter than a polished National Day Rally speech
Several sections feel list-like, moving from example to example without deeper synthesis
Limited emotional resonance and few memorable lines
Conclusion is competent but not especially stirring or unifying
Only partial stylistic resemblance to the intended speaker; the speech is more generic contemporary TED-policy prose than Lee Kuan Yew’s distinctive voice
suggested_improvements: Add stronger political and civic stakes so the speech feels more like a national address than a policy summary
Turn clusters of facts into sharper arguments that explain why each example matters for Singapore’s future
Increase rhetorical punch through stronger contrasts, repetition, and more memorable closing language
Make the conclusion broader and more inspiring by tying AI directly to security, resilience, and national survival or success
For stronger style fidelity, use more blunt, pragmatic phrasing, firmer judgments, and a more forceful problem-to-consequence-to-solution argumentative rhythm
